meta.title: A Historical Audit of “The Great Controversy”
meta.description: 4,443 claims from Ellen G. White's book checked against the work of historians. Full data and method are open.

home.h1: What is left of “The Great Controversy” once you check every fact in it?
home.lede: We copied out 4,443 claims from Ellen G. White's “The Great Controversy” (every date, event, quotation, and figure) and checked everything open to historical verification against the work of historians. The book's checkable fabric is largely intact: 94.8% of its claims held up, not one forgery turned up among its 1,283 quotations, and the Reformation chapters passed with no damage to any claim their story rests on. What the book bears worst is its own categorical voice: the words “all”, “never”, “not one” rarely survived the documents, though behind most such sentences stands a careful version the documents do support. Nearly every error we found, the book inherited from the historical works of its era, along with their knowledge and their blind spots.

stats.claims: claims copied out of the book: dates, events, quotations, figures
stats.supportedShare: of the checkable claims were confirmed by historians
stats.discrepancies: confirmed discrepancies with the historical record
stats.discrepanciesNote: the book inherited 96.9% of them from the works of its era rather than inventing them
stats.adjudicated: disputed questions resolved under rules fixed in advance

chain.intro: The book tells one story in seven steps, from the early church to the end of time. Each step rests on the ones before it. Here is what remains of each after the audit.
chain.hint: Hover over a link to read its outcome; click to open the full review.

5.title: The sanctuary and the investigative judgment

7.title: The final crisis and the resolution

plain.1: The historical base is intact (the persecution, the sign, the window to leave the city), with one load-bearing exception: “not one Christian perished” has no ancient support. The chapter's categorical wording did not hold up.
plain.2: The historical construction is dismantled: the “remnant's” antiquity and its Sabbath find no support, with a millennium-wide gap. The reality of hidden communities, memorized Scripture, and persecution is confirmed.
plain.3: The base is intact and the presentation accurate: the account of Luther, Hus, and Wycliffe is confirmed; only scenes added by later biographers did not hold up, and they carry no weight.
plain.4: The base rests on contested ground: the calculation's anchor itself is contested, and the claim “beyond question” is refuted by the sources. The movement's arithmetic and its history are told honestly for what its era knew.
plain.5: The entire link rests on an interpretation of prophecy, a premise a historian can neither confirm nor refute.
plain.6: The base is mixed: an unbroken Sabbath through the centuries is dismantled, Sunday's origin is a live scholarly dispute; Rome's documented role in suppressing the Sabbath is confirmed, and the presentation is overstated.
plain.7: Almost entirely about the future and faith: history has nothing to check here, and the link's force depends on the ones before it.

1.title: Extract
1.text: Every checkable claim was copied out of every chapter: dates, events, quotations, figures. No judgments, just the complete list.

2.title: Check
2.text: Each claim was checked against the work of modern historians, and separately against the books available to the author in her own time.

3.title: Hear the defense
3.text: A separate pass served as the book's defense attorney: it tried to overturn every accusation. Fifteen accusations it dismissed entirely.

4.title: Resolve the disputes
4.text: 97 questions on which scholars disagree were resolved under rules written down and frozen before the review began.

quotes.title: Not one forged quotation
quotes.text: The book contains 1,283 quotations attributed to historical figures. After every check, not a single confirmed distortion remains; the last contested quotation proved accurate on review.

inherited.title: Errors from the library, not the imagination
inherited.text: 96.9% of the confirmed discrepancies repeat the 19th-century historical works the author relied on. The book erred together with its era: the charge of inventing facts held up in only two peripheral cases.

reformation.title: The Reformation chapters stood
reformation.text: The account of Luther, Hus, and Wycliffe, a third of the book, passed the audit with no damage to any claim the story rests on. Only the ornament failed: legends added by biographers of the 17th–19th centuries.

waldenses.title: Waldensian antiquity did not hold up
waldenses.text: The two pillars of the “faithful remnant” chapter, the Waldenses' apostolic antiquity and an unbroken Sabbath across the centuries, are dismantled by modern research. This is the book's heaviest damage.

absolutes.title: The absolutes did not hold up; the era's substance did
absolutes.text: Of the 81 statements that failed the review, 73 recover once the words “all”, “never”, “only” are removed. The book's rhetoric failed the audit more often than its facts.

pella.title: Jerusalem, AD 70: a template of certainty
pella.text: The book prints that when Jerusalem fell, “not one Christian perished.” No document knows this: whether the Christians left the city at all is still debated. Throughout the book, open questions harden into absolutes this way.

boundary.text: This study passes no judgment on God, prophecy, or faith. 1,346 of the book's claims (about the heavenly sanctuary, angels, the future) lie beyond historical checking and were counted neither true nor false. Ten historical questions remain open: on each, scholarship holds strong arguments on both sides, and for each we recorded what discovery would tip the scales. The book's theological meaning this audit neither confirms nor refutes: it checks history alone.

stub.title: This page is still being assembled
stub.text: The home page is ready; the site's sections open in stages. This section arrives in the next stage.

passage.heading: What the book claims: one passage up close
passage.note: A passage from chapter {ch} (paragraph {p}) of the 1911 edition — one telling example, not the whole chapter: claims that stood and claims that fell sit side by side in it. The link's full picture is in the lists below; hover over a highlight to see the record number.

groups.conditional: What rests on an untestable premise

item.periodScholarship: the era's scholarship said the same

subtraction.heading: Remove what failed: what remains
subtraction.formulaA: Not one checkable link of the book's doctrinal chain held up in the form in which it is printed.
subtraction.formulaB: Nowhere is the doctrinal chain refuted in its careful, internally consistent form; and at its base lies a premise a historian can neither confirm nor refute.
subtraction.outro: Both formulas are true at once: that is the result of the subtraction analysis across all the audit's data. The data does not decide for the reader which one to stress.

1.intro: The book opens with the fall of Jerusalem in AD 70: the city that rejected the warning lost everything, while the Christians who heeded it were saved to the last one. The audit split this account in two. The story itself, the siege, the Romans' sudden withdrawal, the window for escape, stands: partly confirmed, partly honestly open. The absolute “not one perished” turned out to be no ancient testimony but an argument of early-modern commentators.
1.after: Take away the absolute and the chapter barely changes: the persecution, the sign, the window for escape, and the tradition of the community's departure all stay in place. Only “not one” becomes “apparently most,” and the flight to Pella itself becomes a question scholars have debated since 1951.

2.intro: The Waldenses chapter is the book's most damaged place: its whole history of a faithful minority rests on the “faithful remnant's” apostolic antiquity and its Sabbath. The audit separated legend from reality. The secret preachers, the years of memorizing Scripture, the persecution and endurance are documented. The apostolic antiquity, the Sabbath, and the proto-Protestant faith are an inheritance of 19th-century romantic historiography.
2.after: After subtraction the chapter tells a documented story of medieval biblical dissent, persecuted, steadfast, and surviving, but born in the 12th century. The specifically Sabbath-keeping genealogy of the “remnant,” the one the doctrinal chapters rely on, does not recover.

3.intro: The only link with no damage to anything its argument rests on. The account of Wycliffe, Hus, and Luther passed the audit in its factual core; only the ornament did not hold up: scenes added by later biographers, and absolutes like “without the slightest foundation.” Two initial conclusions were reversed in the book's favor on review. This link's soundness is as much a first-class result of the audit as its neighbors' damage.
3.after: There is almost nothing to subtract: remove the legendary layer and the reader loses a few vivid scenes and not one joint of the argument. As an account of the Reformation, the book remains a conscientious popularization of its century's Protestant historiography.

4.intro: The book's axis: the calculation from the decree of 457 BC to 1844. The audit closed the old dispute in both directions. As printed, the chronology did not hold up: the anchor is off by a year, AD 31 is excluded by astronomy, and the formulas “beyond question” are refuted by the very history of the debate. Yet the movement's arithmetic is internally exact for what its era knew, and the Millerites' own history is told accurately, down to one accusation reversed in the book's favor.
4.after: After subtraction what remains is what was always there: an accurate history of the movement and an internally consistent calculation on a chronology the era's scholarship held true and modern scholarship has shifted. What departs is incontestability: the date remains computable on its premises, but ceases to be “fixed beyond question.”

5.intro: The sanctuary link rests not on documents but on an interpretation of prophecy. Its core, that the seventy weeks are “cut off” from the 2300 days and begin together, the review found to be entirely interpretive: history can neither confirm nor refute it. Every checkable input of the link is a repetition of the 457→1844 chronology from chapter 18, and every one inherits the same anchor.
5.after: Remove the chronological repetitions and the link does not vanish; it hangs, honestly, on its premise. A reader who accepts the interpretation receives an internally exact calculation on the data of that era; a reader who does not receives nothing from history that would compel acceptance.

6.intro: The doctrinal link on Sabbath and Sunday. The genealogical absolutes did not hold up: “all Christians kept the Sabbath,” “changed by the papal power,” “Sunday originated with Rome.” The documented core is confirmed: a gradual, many-caused displacement of the Sabbath with Rome playing an active part, the absence of any direct biblical command for Sunday, and the honestly open question of Sunday's apostolic origin. The Sabbath's theological status the audit does not decide.
6.after: After subtraction the link keeps its historical core and loses its genealogy: the Sabbath really did yield to Sunday gradually and for more than one reason, Rome really did suppress the Sabbath for centuries, and Scripture really contains no direct command for Sunday. The absolutes “all,” “changed by Rome,” and “Eden as an event of history” do not recover.

7.intro: The final link looks to the future and speaks the language of faith: history has almost nothing to check here. Of its few checkable details, two failed the review and one remains honestly open. The link has no historical footing of its own; its force depends entirely on the links about 1844, the sanctuary, and the Sabbath.
7.after: Subtraction hardly works here: the link's checkable fabric is thin. What remains depends not on this link itself but on what the reader carries out of the links about 1844, the sanctuary, and the Sabbath.

claimNotes.GC-01-079: “Not one Christian perished”: the formula appears in no ancient author; it arose among 17th–19th-century commentators as an argument from silence. The careful version (“the community mostly escaped”) remains an open question.
claimNotes.GC-01-101: The order to “spare every stone” is absent even from Josephus, Titus's eyewitness apologist: in his account Titus orders the temple gates set on fire.
claimNotes.GC-02-018: “Hunted wherever they fled”: Trajan's rescript expressly forbade seeking Christians out; persecution came in bursts, not as a standing manhunt.
claimNotes.GC-02-041: The generalization that “most Christians lowered the standard” failed; the framing fell, not the fact: the mass influx of nominal converts is documented.
claimNotes.GC-02-053: “The Bible was not accepted as the standard of faith”: refuted by the Christian writers themselves, from Irenaeus to Aquinas.
claimNotes.GC-03-032: “The Sabbath was kept by all Christians”: already in the 2nd century Justin and the Epistle of Barnabas describe Sunday-keeping communities. The careful version, that many, especially in the East, kept the Sabbath alongside Sunday for centuries, is solidly confirmed.
claimNotes.GC-02-050: The pattern “purity under persecution, corruption in prosperity” was vindicated on review: studies of the 4th-century mass conversions support it.
claimNotes.GC-02-038: The influx of former pagans carried their customs into the church: confirmed, including by Eusebius testifying against his own interest.
claimNotes.GC-02-008: “Great numbers” of early martyrs: more likely true; serious modern estimates run to many thousands, against the deflationary revisions.
claimNotes.GC-01-084: The flight to Pella itself is attested only centuries later. Since 1951 scholars have debated its reality, and the debate is unresolved.
claimNotes.GC-04-001: “Witnesses for the truth in every age,” keeping the Sabbath and Scripture alone: a 19th-century construction (Andrews, Wylie); modern medieval scholarship finds no unbroken chain.
claimNotes.GC-04-034: “The Waldenses' faith was their inheritance from the apostles”: the movement arose in the 1170s with Waldo of Lyon. This claim carries the heaviest error weight in the entire book; the “remnant's” genealogy rests on it.
claimNotes.GC-04-046: The inquisition records know no Waldensian Sabbath: the community honored Sunday, and the nickname insabbatati comes from the preachers' sandals.
claimNotes.GC-26-032: “A hidden Sabbath-keeping remnant through the ages”: documented Sabbath-keeping first appears around 1402, a millennium's gap after the apostles.
claimNotes.GC-04-069: Waldensian youth studying in the city schools of France and Italy is a romantic scene after Wylie; the barbes' training was internal, secret, and penitential.
claimNotes.GC-04-082: The proto-Protestant “faith without the merit of works”: the Waldenses rejected purgatory and indulgences, but understood salvation through penance and works of mercy.
claimNotes.GC-04-113: “They sowed the seeds of the Reformation”: Wycliffe's and Luther's Reformation arose independently; the real Bohemian channel of influence is narrow and late.
claimNotes.GC-35-086: “Some of whom were observers of the Sabbath”: the era's documented Sabbath-keepers were the Passagii, not the Waldenses. The single lead, a Bohemian line in a late catalog, remains unexamined by specialists; softening does not rescue it.
claimNotes.GC-15-043: The Albigenses as “brethren in the faith”: traditional scholarship holds the Cathars were dualists, and the revisionists decline to reconstruct their beliefs at all; no school supports the “brotherhood.”
claimNotes.GC-05-107: “He had no predecessors”: the core of Wycliffe's program, the doctrine of dominion, was taken from FitzRalph. The careful version, a synthesis first of its kind, is solidly confirmed.
claimNotes.GC-07-036: The “voice like thunder” on Pilate's staircase is a late family tradition dramatized by D'Aubigné. Luther's climb itself is documented; the staircase awakening remains an open question.
claimNotes.GC-08-088: “Here I stand”: the words appear in the first printing of the 1521 speech but not in the Diet's records; scholars honestly disagree. The substance of Luther's answer at Worms is authentic.
claimNotes.GC-10-054: “Müntzer was driven by ambition”: the documented motive is his own apocalyptic theology; “ambition” goes back to Melanchthon's polemic. What failed here is an attributed motive.
claimNotes.GC-10-066: “Without the slightest foundation”: the absolute did not hold up; the peasants' Twelve Articles speak the language of the gospel. Luther himself did not call for the revolt; the careful version is confirmed.
claimNotes.GC-14-156: Wesley's “more than half a million avowed adherents”: the society rolls of 1791 give 120,000–150,000; several hundred thousand counting the hearers is more likely true.
claimNotes.GC-15-058: “By special order of the king… to all the provinces”: Charles IX's letters ordered the protection of Protestants; La Rochelle, Nîmes, and Montauban were untouched. Massacres in a dozen cities amid rumors of the king's will are confirmed.
claimNotes.GC-06-027: The two Englishmen with their pictures in Prague belong to the layer of Comenius's 1648 martyrology. The antithesis pictures themselves, Christ against the pope, are a documented Hussite motif, but a decade later and with different actors.
claimNotes.GC-11-095: “The emperor declared the Protestant articles to be but the truth”: one of only two confirmed contradictions of the record in the whole book; Charles V adopted the Catholic Confutation. The chapter's argument has confirmed parallels.
claimNotes.GC-18-060: The 457 anchor: cuneiform chronology puts Artaxerxes' seventh year at 458, and the autumn reckoning that rescues 457 is refuted by the Elephantine documents. The careful version, a date within a year of the true one, following the era's standard chronology, is more likely true.
claimNotes.GC-18-071: The crucifixion in AD 31 is excluded by astronomy: a Friday 14 Nisan is possible in 30 and 33. An independent error, not dependent on the 457 anchor.
claimNotes.GC-18-078: “Fixed beyond question”: refuted by the very history of the debate; the calculation was contested by standard chronology, by other schools of interpretation, and by the Millerites themselves in 1844. What failed is the claim to incontestability.
claimNotes.GC-26-069: “Stands without impeachment”: the same absolute of certainty in another chapter, and the same fate.
claimNotes.GC-18-081: The conclusion “the 2300 days terminate in 1844” inherits the 457 anchor. The arithmetic itself is correct; “on the premises of its era the calculation is internally exact” is solidly confirmed.
claimNotes.GC-22-039: The repetition of 457/1844 in chapter 22 inherits the same anchor and counts as the same single error, not a new one.
claimNotes.GC-20-016: “Discovered independently, solely by the study of the Scriptures”: the connections are documented, the Albury conferences, Irving's translation of Lacunza, a shared expository literature. “Similar conclusions with little direct contact” is more likely true.
claimNotes.GC-18-119: Litch's quotation on the fall of Ottoman power is reproduced accurately, with his own hedges. The review reversed the initial doubt: a ruling in the book's favor.
claimNotes.GC-18-062: “The decree in its completest form”: the reading of three decrees combined in Ezra 6:14 is vindicated as more likely true.
claimNotes.GC-18-058: The link's core, that the seventy weeks are “cut off” from the 2300 days and begin together, is entirely an interpretation of prophecy: history can neither confirm nor refute it. Without it, not one date of the chain can be computed.
claimNotes.GC-23-008: The 2300 days beginning in the autumn of 457 BC: a repetition of chapter 18's anchor in the sanctuary chapter.
claimNotes.GC-23-009: The baptism “in AD 27” inherits the same chronology: the reckoning of Tiberius's years it rests on has been revised.
claimNotes.GC-23-010: The crucifixion “in the spring of AD 31”: a repetition of the date excluded by astronomical reconstruction.
claimNotes.GC-23-012: “From AD 34, 1810 years extend to 1844”: the arithmetic is correct; the date inherits the shared anchor.
claimNotes.GC-26-014: “The Sabbath was changed by the papal power”: the displacement was gradual, many-caused, and in large part Eastern; the canon of Laodicea, Constantine's law of 321. Rome's active role in suppressing the Sabbath is more likely true.
claimNotes.GC-26-025: “Changed by the Roman power”: shares one root with the previous claim and counts as the same error.
claimNotes.GC-35-057: “Sunday observance originated with Rome”: the earliest first-day evidence is geographically scattered, Alexandria, Asia Minor, before any Roman initiative. Rome's leading role in suppressing the Sabbath is more likely true.
claimNotes.GC-25-051: The Sabbath “from Eden” as an event of primeval history: no culture before Israel knows a seven-day week. “An ancient institution which the biblical tradition itself traces to creation” is solidly confirmed.
claimNotes.GC-25-126: “The papacy dropped the second commandment and cut down the fourth”: Catholic catechisms follow the Augustinian numbering of the commandments rather than “dropping” them. The formula repeats 19th-century polemic.
claimNotes.GC-25-131: “It was in behalf of Sunday that popery first resorted to the power of the state”: Rome's leading role in exalting Sunday has support, but the first act of state power was Constantine's law of 321, not a papal initiative.
claimNotes.GC-03-050: Whether Sunday worship goes back to the apostles themselves is an open question: the debate runs across confessional lines.
claimNotes.GC-35-103: Sunday has no direct biblical command; its defenders concede as much. The comparison of Protestant teachers with “papal leaders” did not hold as printed; the substance is solidly confirmed.
claimNotes.GC-42-032: The massacre of the innocents: the account exists only in Matthew, and Josephus is silent; scholars honestly disagree, with serious specialists defending its plausibility.
claimNotes.GC-36-044: The soul's immortality as a “papal error”: the teaching entered Christianity from Greek philosophy in the fathers of the 2nd–5th centuries, long before the papacy. “The only defense against Spiritualism” is an exclusive that did not hold.
claimNotes.GC-37-029: “The Scriptures are withheld from the people,” in the present tense of 1911: by then Rome encouraged the reading of approved translations. The centuries-old restrictive core of the past is documented.
claimNotes.GC-05-075: “The first English translation of the Bible”: the first round filed this as an error of its era; the defense vindicated the wording, for no complete English translation existed before Wycliffe.
claimNotes.GC-12-027: “The king's sister accepted the reformed faith”: Marguerite of Navarre really did patronize the reform and share its ideas; the defense withdrew the initial objection.
claimNotes.GC-14-032: The Bishop of Durham bought a bookseller's whole stock of Tyndale's Bibles to destroy them, and unwittingly financed a new edition: the first round called the story contradicted; the defense vindicated it.

dossiersPage.intro: The first round of checking left 97 of the book's claims with a live scholarly dispute on their hands. We grouped them into 23 questions and reviewed each under rules written down and frozen before the work began. Every claim received two rulings, the text as printed and a careful version without “all” and “never”; for each, the strongest opposing argument was engaged, and what discovery would change the conclusion was recorded. Below is the open tally of rulings and all 23 reviews.

strip.gapTitle: The gap between wording and substance
strip.gapSub: Across all 97 claims: 0 means the printed text and the careful version fare the same, 4 means the categorical text did not hold up while its substance is solidly confirmed. The peak at 3–4 means the wording failed more often than the facts.
strip.dirSub: The tally was kept openly and in both directions; any drift would be visible.
strip.chainsNote: The 81 rulings against the printed text amount to 68 independent errors: the other 13 repeat one shared root and are counted with it.
strip.reversalsNote: Two first-round conclusions were reversed in the book's favor on review: the accuracy of Litch's quotation and the reality of the Bohemian persecutions before Hus.

scale.title: Ruling scale
scale.steps: refuted · more likely false · scholars disagree · more likely true · solidly confirmed

card.outOfScope: rests entirely on an untestable premise

questions.D01: Is the calculation from the decree of 457 BC to 1844 correct, and was it ever “beyond question”?
questions.D02: When, where, and by whose will did Sunday displace the Sabbath?
questions.D03: Did the advent message arise independently in different countries, and did Litch's prediction come true on the appointed day?
questions.D04: Were the three and a half years of Revelation 11 fulfilled in the French Revolution?
questions.D05: Is it true that not one Christian perished in the fall of Jerusalem?
questions.D06: Has the Sabbath existed since Eden?
questions.D07: Did Rome hide the Bible from the people, right up to 1911?
questions.D08: Where did the doctrines of the immortal soul and eternal torment come from: from Rome?
questions.D09: What is true in the Luther scenes: “Here I stand,” the voice on the staircase, Müntzer's ambition?
questions.D10: Did the church merge with paganism after Constantine?
questions.D11: Were the medieval principles of the papacy still in force in 1911?
questions.D12: Who were the Waldenses and Albigenses: proto-Protestants or medieval dissenters?
questions.D13: Is the book's chronological frame sound: Moses, 1600 years, “the first 2500 years of history”?
questions.D14: Did the persecuted Waldenses keep the Sabbath?
questions.D15: How many died in the Lisbon earthquake of 1755?
questions.D16: Did Titus try to save the temple, or did he order it burned?
questions.D17: What was the real scale of the persecution of early Christians?
questions.D18: Did the apostate power have “millions of martyrs”?
questions.D19: Did Wesley have half a million avowed adherents?
questions.D20: Did the St. Bartholomew massacre spread “by special order of the king” to all the provinces?
questions.D21: Was the massacre of the innocents at Bethlehem a historical event?
questions.D22: In the Bohemian prologue of the Hus story, what is history and what is legend?
questions.D23: Was Wycliffe a reformer without predecessors?

narratives.D01: This dossier examines the arithmetical chain from 457 BC to 1844 on which the book's entire Millerite-Adventist chronology rests. The arithmetic itself is correct throughout, but the final dates as printed hang on a contested anchor: cuneiform chronology puts the seventh year of Artaxerxes I at 458, not 457; astronomical reconstructions exclude a crucifixion in AD 31; and the baptism “in the autumn of AD 27” relies on a now-rejected reckoning of Tiberius's years. The formulas “fixed beyond question” and “stands without impeachment” contradict the documented picture: the calculation was contested both by contemporary scholars and by the Millerites themselves in 1844. At the same time the softened versions of nearly every claim hold up: 457 was the standard biblical chronology of the era (the margins of King James Bibles), the events around AD 34 (Stephen's martyrdom, the persecution) fall within the scholarly window, and the calculation is internally consistent. The gap between the rhetoric of certainty and the defensible substance is the dossier's main finding: what suffers is not so much the factual basis of the era as the absolute formulations of 1911. The picture would change with documents showing an autumn reckoning of Persian regnal years among Jewish scribes, or new data on the calendar of Judea in the 20s–30s of the first century.
narratives.D02: This dossier examined eight of the book's claims about when, where, and by whose will Sunday displaced the Sabbath in early Christianity. In the author's formulations nearly all of them failed: attributing the change to “the papal power,” deriving Sunday “from Rome,” and making the papal palace the source of the forged “heavenly scroll” contradict the dating and geography of the sources, and the thesis that “the Sabbath was kept by all Christians” is refuted by Justin and the Epistle of Barnabas as early as the 2nd century. The weakened versions of the same theses proved solid: the Roman church demonstrably suppressed the Sabbath (the Sabbath fast, the decretal of Innocent I), Catholic authors from the Council of Trent to 19th-century catechisms themselves called the change of day the work of the church's authority, and there is no direct biblical command for Sunday, as its defenders concede. Whether Sunday worship goes back to the apostles or arose after them remains open among specialists across confessional lines. The picture would change with finds on the order of a 2nd-century Roman decree on the day of assembly, chancery documents on the scroll of 1201, or evidence that bishops prompted Constantine's law of 321.
narratives.D03: This dossier examines two episodes of Millerite history: the thesis that the advent message arose in different countries independently, “solely by the study of the Scriptures,” and the story of Josiah Litch's prediction of the fall of Ottoman power on August 11, 1840. The book reproduces Litch's quotation accurately, with his own hedges and correct dating, so that claim was moved to the confirmed. The “fulfilled appointed day” itself does not survive checking: the Porte accepted the powers' protection by the convention of July 15, the ultimatum reached Muhammad Ali on August 16–19, and on August 11 only a steamer carrying an envoy arrived in Alexandria; Litch himself later abandoned the interpretation. The thesis of a faith arising “solely from Scripture” in isolated groups contradicts documented connections: Irving's translation of Lacunza, the Albury conferences with Wolff's participation, a shared expository tradition; in the weakened form (“similar conclusions with little direct contact,” as with Bengel, Lacunza, and Miller) the picture is plausible. The rulings would shift with an Ottoman document recording a formal act of submission precisely on August 11, or archival evidence of groups reaching a dated advent expectation outside these networks and this literature.
narratives.D04: This dossier examines three claims of the French Revolution chapter: an interval of exactly three and a half years between the abolition of religion in 1793 and its restoration “by the same body,” the slogan “crush the wretch” as aimed personally at Christ, and the words “after me the deluge” in the mouth of Louis XV. The legislative record refutes the first construction: the Convention reaffirmed freedom of worship as early as December 6, 1793; the law of 3 Ventôse (February 21, 1795) restored free exercise of religion after fifteen months; and by June 1797 the Convention no longer existed, so “the same body” could repeal nothing. Voltaire scholars (Besterman, Peter Gay, Pomeau) read “l'infâme” as superstition, fanaticism, and the persecuting church; the reading “that is, Christ” has no scholarly support. The earliest source attributes the deluge phrase to Madame de Pompadour, and already in 1756 Mirabeau called it a common saying. In their weakened versions all three claims are sound: the dechristianization campaign with public burnings of Bibles, the anti-Christian thrust of Voltaire's slogan, and the careless fatalism of Louis XV's court are documented. The picture would change with specific acts of 1793 and 1797 found in the parliamentary archives, a letter of Voltaire applying “l'infâme” to Christ himself, or a contemporary record of the king's words.
narratives.D05: This dossier reviews the book's account of the Christians' fate in the fall of Jerusalem: not one Christian perished, the community fled to Pella beyond Jordan, and the window for flight was opened by Cestius Gallus's withdrawal in 66. Josephus describes Cestius's withdrawal and the pursuit of his army in detail, and prominent residents were indeed leaving the city at that moment. The community's flight to Pella is attested only by Eusebius and Epiphanius centuries later; since Brandon's book of 1951 historians have disputed its reality (the skeptics Lüdemann and Verheyden against the defenders of the tradition's core, Koester and Bourgel), and the dispute is unresolved. The absolute “not one perished” occurs in no ancient author: Eusebius does not assert it; the formula was born among 17th–19th-century commentators (Hammond, Newton, Clarke) as an argument from silence and entered the book almost verbatim from that tradition. As written, the zero-casualties claim was ruled improbable; the weakened versions (the community mostly escaped; the window for departure was real) stand between genuinely open and well supported. The picture would change with finds of first-century Judeo-Christian refugees at Pella or a pre-Eusebian source on the fate of the Jerusalem community.
narratives.D06: This dossier examined one load-bearing claim: that the Sabbath has existed as an institution from creation itself, from Eden. Documented history does not confirm this: no people before Israel attests either a seven-day week or a weekly rest day, and Genesis itself mentions no Sabbath-keeping by anyone from Adam to Moses. Academic scholarship traces the Sabbath as an Israelite institution which the creation account grounds retrospectively; the defense of an Edenic origin rests on a theological reading of the text, not on historical evidence. The weakened version stood: the Sabbath is indeed an ancient institution, and the biblical tradition itself connects it with creation as its memorial from the start. The theological thesis that God instituted the Sabbath, and to what end, remains outside historical checking. The picture would change with the discovery of a pre-Israelite monument attesting a continuous seven-day cycle with a recurring rest day.
narratives.D07: This dossier examines six of the book's claims that Rome hid the Bible from the people, from the Middle Ages down to the present tense of 1911. In the author's formulations not one stood: no single universal policy of concealment existed, at least 18 complete Bibles were printed in Germany before Luther, and by 1911 the popes themselves encouraged the reading of approved translations, up to an indulgence for daily Gospel reading (1898). At the same time the weakened versions of all six claims are confirmed: Rome restricted lay access to vernacular Scripture for centuries; after the Council of Trent an almost total ban operated in Italy and Spain, documented from the archives of the Inquisition (Fragnito); and the church officially forbade independent interpretation of Scripture. The dossier's main finding is precisely this gap: the absolute rhetoric is false, the restrictive core is documented. The picture would change with a conciliar decree of church-wide force ordering the concealment of Scripture, or proof that the permissions of 1897–1911 remained a dead letter throughout the church.
narratives.D08: This dossier examines the pedigree of two doctrines: eternal torment and the natural immortality of the soul. Historians of dogma and Adventist authors themselves (Froom) agree that the concept of a naturally immortal soul entered Christian theology from Greek philosophy. The book, however, credits this borrowing to Rome, whereas the assimilation happened in the fathers of the 2nd–5th centuries (Athenagoras, Tertullian, Origen, Augustine), centuries before the medieval papacy, and the Eastern churches hold the same teachings without Roman mediation. All three claims in the author's formulation were ruled improbable, while their weakened versions (the Protestants' inheritance of these doctrines through the medieval Latin church, Rome's later dogmatization in 1336 and 1513, the Protestants' vulnerability to Spiritualism) stood at well supported and probable. The picture would change with documents showing a specifically Roman channel for the introduction of these doctrines into early Christianity.
narratives.D09: This dossier gathers four episodes of the Luther story which The Great Controversy retells after D'Aubigné. The famous phrase “Here I stand” appears in the first printed edition of the 1521 speech but is absent from the Diet's records, and scholars still differ on whether Luther spoke it; the substance of his answer at Worms is authentic. The scene on the Holy Stairs with the “voice like thunder” and the characterization of Müntzer as ambitious did not survive checking as written: the first rests on a late family tradition dramatized by D'Aubigné, the second contradicts Müntzer's documented apocalyptic-theological motives. The absolute denial of “the slightest foundation” for blaming Luther in the Peasants' War likewise diverges from modern historiography, though Luther himself condemned the revolt and did not call for it. In weakened formulations three of the four claims are well confirmed, and the picture would change with new 16th-century documents: an independent witness to the words at Worms, an early record of the staircase episode, or a letter of Müntzer with careerist motives.
narratives.D10: This dossier reviews the picture of the church's “Constantinian fall”: the merging of Christianity with paganism after the persecutions ceased, the substitution of saints' images for idols, and the loss of the Bible as the standard of faith. The factual core held: scholars (MacMullen, Brown, Frankfurter) acknowledge the mass influx of superficially converted pagans and the transfer of many functions of pagan cult to the cult of the saints. The book's total formulations did not pass: the theses of “paganism victorious,” of the majority of Christians lowering their standard, and of purgatory as an “invention of paganism” diverge from the balance of specialist research, and the words that the Bible was not accepted as the standard of faith are refuted by the texts of the church fathers and medieval theologians. In weakened versions seven of the eight claims were rated well supported and one probable, so the dossier's main finding is the gap between rhetoric and factual base. The picture would change with period documents showing the deliberate introduction of image veneration to attract pagans, or an official renunciation by the church of that era of the authority of Scripture.
narratives.D11: This dossier examines three claims about the immutability of papal doctrine: that the principles of Gregory VII and Innocent III remained the principles of Rome in 1911, that the church proclaims the rule that “faith is not to be kept with heretics,” and that medieval laypeople were taught to see in the pope the sole earthly mediator on the way to God. The medieval documentary core of each claim is real: the Dictatus Papae asserted the right to depose emperors, the bull Unam Sanctam declared submission to the pope necessary for salvation, and the Council of Constance ruled on September 23, 1415 that a safe-conduct does not protect a heretic from the church's court, which cost Hus his life. As written, all three claims were ruled improbable: by 1911 the church was not asserting the deposing power as operative, its official authors expressly denied the maxim about heretics, and laypeople were taught to approach God through Christ, the sacraments, and the saints, not through the pope personally. The weakened versions are rated well supported: the council really issued such a decree and never revoked it, Rome never renounced its medieval claims and in 1906 still condemned the separation of church and state, and medieval doctrine did declare submission to the pope a condition of salvation. The picture would change with early 20th-century documents in which Rome asserted the deposition of rulers or the maxim about heretics as operative teaching.
narratives.D12: This dossier examines the book's portrait of the Waldenses and Albigenses: students with manuscripts in specially made clothing, a proto-Protestant soteriology, “the seeds of the Reformation,” and the Albigenses as “brethren in the faith.” Modern scholars (Cameron, Audisio, Biller) confirm the documented core of these scenes: secret itinerant preachers, the barbes, years of memorizing Scripture, artisan covers, the rejection of indulgences and purgatory, martyrdom in Piedmont. But the book's specific details (study in the city schools of France and Italy, specially prepared clothing, total elusiveness before the Inquisition, faith in salvation without the merit of works) go back to 19th-century romantic historiography, whereas the barbes' training was internal and penitential, and the Reformation of Wycliffe and Luther arose independently of the Waldenses. Calling the Albigenses the Waldenses' “brethren” is supported neither by traditional scholarship, which holds the Cathars were dualists, nor by revisionists like Pegg and Moore, for whom Albigensian beliefs cannot be reconstructed at all. In weakened formulations nearly all the dossier's claims are confirmed, and whether medieval piety was oppression without comfort remains honestly open: the Ozment–Duffy debate continues. The picture would change with finds such as inquisition records of Waldensian youth studying at universities, or a corpus of Albigensian texts with a biblical, non-dualist faith.
narratives.D13: This dossier examines the book's frame for the composition of the Bible: written revelation began with Moses, the Bible was written over 1600 years from Moses to John, Joel wrote 2500 years before 1780, and the warning of Deuteronomy sounded fourteen centuries before the destruction of Jerusalem in AD 70. Modern biblical scholarship dates the oldest biblical books to the first millennium BC and ties the core of Deuteronomy to the seventh century, so four of the five claims as written were rated from improbable to discredited; only the frame of “the first 2500 years of human history,” which requires humanity to begin around 4000 BC, was discredited outright. The dating of Joel remains an honestly open question: specialists propose dates from the ninth to the fourth century BC, and the figure “2500 years” falls inside that range. In weakened versions (the Bible took shape over about a millennium and more; Deuteronomy's warning is many centuries older than AD 70) four of the five claims are well confirmed. The picture would change with datable manuscripts or inscriptions of the second millennium BC carrying biblical text.
narratives.D14: This dossier examined one aside from chapter 35: that among the Waldenses persecuted by Rome “some were observers of the Sabbath.” The persecutions themselves are fully confirmed, but modern medieval scholarship rejects Waldensian Sabbath-keeping: their nickname insabbatati derives from the preachers' distinctive sandals, and the rich inquisition records describe a community that honored Sunday and rejected church festivals. The arguments for the claim go back to 17th-century glosses and to a conflation of the Waldenses with the Lombard sect of the Passagii, who really did keep the Sabbath; Adventist reference works themselves concede the weakness of these arguments. The only direct document, a hostile 15th-century catalog of errors from Bohemia with the words “some celebrate the Sabbath with the Jews,” leaves open only a strongly weakened variant about the movement's late Bohemian periphery. As written the claim was ruled improbable; the weakened version remains an open question until specialists examine the Bohemian document.
narratives.D15: Chapter 17 describes the Lisbon earthquake of 1755 with figures from 19th-century reference works: 60,000 dead in six minutes, 90,000 in a day, and a marble quay that sank with everyone on it. Modern studies (Pereira, Chester, Araújo) count 30,000–40,000 victims in Lisbon and 40,000–50,000 across the region, so both of the book's numbers are roughly double. The story of the Cais de Pedra quay has a real core: the pier did subside through soil liquefaction and the people sheltering on it died, but the layer of “every one of them, not one body ever floated up” historians assign to eyewitness exaggeration. In weakened formulations (“tens of thousands dead,” “the quay collapsed and many died”) all three claims are confirmed. White, moreover, quotes her sources accurately (Lyell and the Encyclopaedia Americana of 1831); it is the sources that have aged, not her retelling. The picture would change with a demographic study of the parish registers confirming losses close to 90,000.
narratives.D16: This dossier reviews the scene of the temple's destruction in AD 70: did Titus want to save the sanctuary, as the eyewitness Josephus writes, or did he order it burned, as the tradition of Sulpicius Severus asserts, probably going back to a lost book of Tacitus? Scholars have argued about this since 1861: the majority after Bernays distrusts Josephus's pro-imperial version, yet serious specialists defend it, including Rajak and Leoni. Three of the four claims therefore received the ruling genuinely open: scholarship today cannot decide whether Titus tried to stop the fire, though the massacre of those sheltering in the temple and the assault itself are beyond doubt. The fourth claim, that Titus from the Mount of Olives ordered “not one stone” of the temple touched, is improbable in its literal form: no such order exists even in Josephus, where Titus orders the temple gates set alight. In its weakened form this claim too returns to the open category, since it rests on an eyewitness testimony. The picture would change with the discovery of the lost part of Tacitus's Histories or new independent testimony about Titus's war council.
narratives.D17: This dossier examined five claims of chapter 2 about the scale and mechanics of the persecution of early Christians. Modern scholarship sees sporadic local persecution with a few short empire-wide campaigns in the third and early fourth centuries, so the image of a relentless hunt “wherever they sought refuge” is refuted by Trajan's rescript, which expressly forbade seeking Christians out, and the explanation that paganism “foresaw the triumph of the gospel” remains undocumented. The claim of “great numbers” of martyrs, by contrast, largely stood: serious modern estimates run from several thousand executed upward, though the revisionist line of Candida Moss disputes the traditional scale. The catacombs served as cemeteries and as meeting places in times of prohibition, but not as a “home” for those in hiding, contrary to the 19th-century romantic legend. In weakened formulations tied to the actually documented campaigns of persecution, all five claims are rated from probable to well supported; the picture would change with new documentary finds, such as a pre-imperial edict ordering a universal search for Christians.
narratives.D18: This dossier examined the phrase of chapter 3 about “millions of martyrs” crying for vengeance against the apostate power. Archival counts of executions for heresy give a different order of magnitude: the tribunals of the Inquisition in Spain, Portugal, and Italy together executed on the order of ten or fifteen thousand people across all their centuries, and William Monter estimates all executions for heresy in Europe in 1520–1565 at about three thousand. Even adding the St. Bartholomew massacre, the Waldensian pogroms, and the victims of the Albigensian crusade, an honest total stays within the hundreds of thousands and falls short of “millions” by at least an order of magnitude. As written the formulation was ruled improbable; the weakened version, tens of thousands executed under papal sanction, is well confirmed. The figure itself descends from 19th-century Protestant martyrology: John Dowling in 1845 wrote of fifty million, against which “millions” sounded almost restrained. The picture would change with a peer-reviewed demographic study documenting about two million killed specifically for their faith rather than in religious wars generally.
narratives.D19: This dossier examines one figure from chapter 14: that at the close of Wesley's life his “avowed adherents” numbered more than half a million. The documented rolls of the Methodist societies for 1791 give about 120,000–150,000 members in Britain and America, and academic estimates of the wider circle of regular hearers (Field, the standard biographies by Rack and Heitzenrater) reach 350,000–450,000. Half a million is reachable only with the most stretched multiplier and by substituting unenrolled hearers for those who had openly professed, so the book's formulation was ruled improbable. The weakened version, adherents together with hearers numbering several hundred thousand, was found probable; the figure itself repeats a commonplace of 19th-century Methodist literature. The picture would change with a peer-reviewed count or a contemporary document recording at least half a million professed Methodists in 1791.
narratives.D20: This dossier examined the claim of The Great Controversy that the St. Bartholomew massacre spread “by special order of the king” to all the provinces and towns where Protestants lived. The surviving letters of Charles IX of August 24 and 28, 1572 instructed governors to keep the peace and protect Protestants, while the massacre flared in about a dozen cities through the efforts of local Catholic activists who believed rumors of the king's will (Benedict, Holt, Jouanna). The largest Huguenot cities, including La Rochelle, Nîmes, and Montauban, were untouched, and several governors flatly refused to carry out the supposed order. The book's formulation is rejected as written, since both “the special order of the king” and the coverage of “all the provinces” contradict the documents; the weakened version, that the massacre spread to the provinces and that many perpetrators believed themselves instruments of the royal will, is firmly confirmed. White follows Wylie almost verbatim, who conveyed the standard 19th-century reading; the picture would change if a genuine royal order for the extermination of Protestants in the provinces were found in the archives.
narratives.D21: This dossier examined a single claim: Herod the Great's order to slaughter the infants of Bethlehem as a historical event. The account survives only in the Gospel of Matthew; Josephus, who catalogued Herod's cruelties in detail, is silent, and most critical scholars (Brown, Luz, Vermes) consider the episode legendary or unverifiable. A minority of qualified scholars (France, Maier) defends its plausibility: the killing of a dozen infants in a small village fits Herod's documented behavior in his last years and could have escaped the surviving sources. Both sides work from the same scant material, so the ruling on the book's formulation is: the question is genuinely open. The weakened version (an event of this scale is plausible but unprovable) is rated probable. The picture would change with an independent non-Christian record from the first century, or proof that the whole episode is derived from Jewish traditions about Moses.
narratives.D22: The nine claims of this dossier retell the Bohemian prologue of the Hus chapter after the Victorian book of Wylie, who himself relied on Comenius's martyrology of 1648. Historians confirm the core of every scene: the 14th-century Inquisition really did execute Bohemian Waldenses, Wycliffite ideas really did travel from England to Prague, and contrasting images of the humble Christ and the pompous pope really were displayed in Hussite Prague, from the walls of Bethlehem Chapel to the tables of Nicholas of Dresden. But the book's specific scenes, including the kneeling mother at the gates of Prague and the two English artists with crowds at their pictures, have not a single medieval witness and are absent from Hus's modern biographers, from Spinka to Fudge. As written, therefore, all nine claims were ruled improbable. In weakened versions four rose to solidly confirmed, two to probable, two remained open, and one cannot be softened at all. A 15th-century chronicle of the English visitors, or any document about Hus's family, would change the picture.
narratives.D23: This dossier examines a sentence from chapter 5: that Wycliffe had no predecessors from whose works he could build his system of reform. Read literally this is refuted by the documents: the doctrine of dominion, the backbone of his program, Wycliffe took from FitzRalph's treatise De pauperie Salvatoris, and he constantly cited Grosseteste and Bradwardine as his teachers. The claim as written was therefore ruled improbable. The weakened version fares differently: before Wycliffe England had neither an organized reform movement nor a ready program of reform, and scholarship recognizes his synthesis centered on Scripture in the vernacular as the first of its kind, so this version is rated well supported. The picture would change with proof that Wycliffe developed the dominion theory independently of FitzRalph, or the discovery of an organized English reform movement before him.

chainAssess.mixedDetail6: the Sabbath's continuity is dismantled; Sunday's origin is contested

mapPage.intro: Each column is a chapter: its height shows how many claims we copied out of it, the colors show how each claim fared, and the hatching marks what lies beyond historical checking. The asymmetry is visible at once: the Reformation chapters are almost entirely blue, while the warm tones gather over the Waldenses (chapter 4) and the 1844 chronology (chapters 18 and 23).
mapPage.hint: Hover over a column for the chapter's outcome; click to open all its claims in the table.
mapPage.openChapter: Open this chapter in the table

chapterTitles.15: The Bible and the French Revolution

explorerPage.intro: The complete table: every extracted claim with its quotation, outcome, and audit history. Expand a row to see the verdict's path — the source check, then the defense, then the review of the dispute — with reasoning and sources at every step. Filters are stored in the page address, so a link can be shared.
explorerPage.loadError: The data failed to load. Refresh the page.
explorerPage.defenseFailed: the defense did not persuade; the conclusion stood

methodPage.intro: Here is the whole method: the four-stage pipeline, the built-in safeguards against bias, the rules frozen before the review, the method's limits, the tools, and the open data. The study was carried out with AI under the author's methodology; everything needed to re-check it is open.

pipeline.long1: Every checkable claim was copied out of each of the 43 chapters: dates, events, quotations, figures, causal links. Nothing is judged at this stage — just a complete, neutral list, each record with its exact quotation and address: chapter and paragraph.
pipeline.long2: Each checkable claim was compared against the work of modern historians — and separately against the books available to the author in her own time, because an error of the era and an invention are different things. An accusatory conclusion had to rest on at least two independent sources, and on academic works for the central topics.
pipeline.long3: A separate pass served as the book's defense attorney: it tried to overturn every accusation with its own research — looking for a stronger reading and checking whether the verifier had erred. No accusatory conclusion enters the results without surviving the defense.
pipeline.long4: Questions on which scholars genuinely disagree went to a separate review under rules written down and frozen before it began. Every claim received two rulings — as printed and in a careful version; the other side's strongest argument was engaged in writing; and for every conclusion it was recorded what discovery would change it.
pipeline.stat4: {n} disputed questions resolved on the dual scale

objectivity.text: A study carried out with AI cannot just promise honesty — it has to build honesty into the procedure and publish the tally. The two main safeguards: a mandatory defense of every accusation, and an open distribution of the review's rulings.
objectivity.defenseText: The defense withdrew {full} accusations entirely — including the story of the Bishop of Durham buying up Tyndale's Bibles and Wycliffe's first English Bible — and softened {down} more. After it, not one confirmed misquotation remained in the book.
objectivity.directionTitle: Which way the review's rulings moved
objectivity.directionText: The tally was kept openly and in both directions; there were no quotas, only rules.

charter.text: The 97 disputed questions were reviewed not at discretion but under a charter — a document written, agreed, and frozen before the review began, so it could not be bent toward a desired result. Its full text is in the open data. The main rules:

rules.1: Every claim receives two rulings: the text as printed, and a careful version without “all”, “never”, “only”. The gap between them is a finding in its own right.
rules.2: The more categorical the sentence, the stricter the demand: one well-documented exception refutes “all” and “not one”.
rules.3: The strongest argument of the opposing side is engaged in writing and on the merits; the confession of the argument's author is not an argument.
rules.4: Every conclusion ends with a record of “what would change this conclusion”: what find — a document, a manuscript, a study — would tip the scales.
rules.5: Interpretations of prophecy are never put on the scales: a claim that is theological through and through receives the category “conditional premise” — neither true nor false.

charter.chains: One more rule of honest arithmetic: repetitions of one error are not counted as new errors. The 81 rulings against the printed text amount to 68 independent errors; the other 13 repeat one shared root (for example, ten dates inherit the single 457 BC anchor) and are published only together with that number.

boundary.h2: What the study does not decide
boundary.text: The audit passes no judgment on God, prophecy, or faith: 1,346 of the book's theological claims were counted neither true nor false, and ten historical questions remain honestly open — for each, what discovery would tip the scales is on record. The method also has limits worth stating plainly:

limits.1: The search budget per question was capped; where sources ran short, the record says “scholars disagree”, not a guess.
limits.2: English-language scholarship took priority; some works are covered through surveys and reviews of them.
limits.3: The executors are language models, and they can err. That is why every conclusion carries its sources and full history: the route to re-checking is open to anyone.
limits.4: What was checked is the historical fabric of one book in its 1911 edition — not Adventist theology and not the author's person.

faq.a1: The methodology and the verification pipeline were designed by the author, Vladislav Osin (see “From the author” below); the execution was carried out by Claude models. This is one person's study with AI executors; no organization stands behind it.
faq.q2: Can a study carried out by AI be trusted?
faq.a2: Trust belongs not to the executor but to checkability. The rules were frozen before the work began; every accusation survived an adversarial defense; the tally of rulings is published in both directions; and every conclusion records what discovery would change it. All primary materials are open — an error can be found and pointed out, which is stronger than any promise.
faq.a3: The data.zip archive on this page: the full history of all 4,443 claims, all 23 reviews, the frozen rules, and a bilingual description of the data schema. The site's and pipeline's code is on GitHub.

tools.sonnetRole: extracting claims from the chapters and scoring the weight of errors
tools.opusRole: checking against sources and the adversarial defense
tools.fableRole: the review of disputed questions, the synthesis, and this website
tools.designed: The author designed the methodology and the verification pipeline; the models worked under his rules.

data.text: Everything this site stands on can be downloaded and re-checked: the full history of each of the 4,443 claims, all 23 reviews, the frozen rules, and a bilingual description of the data schema. The dataset is assembled by the same script that produces the site's numbers, so they cannot diverge.
data.provenance: Data version {version}, {date} · research data: CC BY 4.0 · the 1911 English text of the book is public domain

author.text: My name is Vladislav Osin. I am a master's student in machine learning, and I have spent three years engineering neural-network solutions. I am a Christian, a Seventh-day Adventist; for many years I have studied the Bible, church history, and its sources. This study grew out of a desire to answer one question honestly: does the historical fabric of one of the most influential Adventist books withstand checking against modern sources — is there room in it for conjecture, was history bent to fit a desired meaning, or did the author follow the knowledge of her era in good faith? I designed the methodology and the verification pipeline; the execution was carried out by Claude models (listed below); the data, the adjudication charter, and all conclusions are open for verification.

license.text: The research data and the site's texts: CC BY 4.0 © 2026 Vladislav Osin — use freely with attribution. The English text of “The Great Controversy”, 1911 edition, is public domain. The seven excerpts of the official Russian translation on the chain pages are quoted with attribution (© Ellen G. White Estate) and are not part of the dataset.
license.attribution: Attribution example: Osin V., “A Historical Audit of The Great Controversy”, 2026, [URL]

glossary.intro: The site's pages speak plain language; here are the internal pipeline terms you will meet in the downloadable dataset.

1.plain: contradicted by the historical record (two remained after the defense)

2.plain: an error of its era: repeats 19th-century sources that scholarship has since revised

3.plain: scholars disagree; the question went to the review under frozen rules

4.plain: a distorted quotation (none left after the defense)

5.plain: beyond historical checking: theology, the supernatural, the future

8.plain: scholars genuinely disagree: strong arguments on both sides

11.plain: a conditional premise: rests on an interpretation of prophecy that history cannot test

12.plain: the dual ruling: the text as printed, and a careful version without “all” and “never”

13.plain: the gap between wording and substance, from 0 to 4

14.plain: what failed: the fact, the framing, or an attributed motive

15.plain: a load-bearing claim: the chapter's or the book's argument rests on it

16.plain: a repeat of one root: counted as one error together with its anchor

17.plain: what the era's sources said: its scholarship (period-scholarship) or only the expositor tradition (expositor-tradition)
